column13: Istanbul Travel Guide Turkey Travel Guide
column14: 5 things I did wrong in Istanbul 🇹🇷 avoid these mistakes to save money & stress 💸
column15: 1️⃣ Thinking your credit card will work everywhere 💳 Not all places accept cards, and even if they do, some foreign cards (like Dutch ones) might not work! Always carry some cash (Turkish Lira) for local markets, street food, and small shops.  2️⃣ Skipping the Asian side of Istanbul 🏙️ Most tourists stay on the European side, but the Asian side is a MUST! Top 5 places you shouldn't miss: ⭐ Üsküdar - Beautiful Bosphorus views & historical mosques. ⭐ Kuzguncuk - A colorful, charming neighborhood with amazing cafes. ⭐ Kadıköy - The heart of Istanbul's alternative scene, with epic food! ⭐ Çamlıca Hill - The best panoramic view of the entire city. ⭐ Moda - Chill seaside area with parks & cozy bars.  3️⃣ Only visiting Grand Bazaar for shopping 🛍️ Grand Bazaar is fun for the experience (it's one of the world's biggest markets!), but prices are often too high! For better deals on souvenirs, check out: 🛒 Spice Bazaar (Egyptian Bazaar) - Best for Turkish delights, spices & teas. 🛒 İstiklal Street - Modern shopping + local boutiques. 🛒 Arasta Bazaar - Less touristy, more authentic finds!  4️⃣ Not getting an eSIM or the right SIM card 📶 Public Wi-Fi is rare in Istanbul! Instead of paying crazy prices at the airport, get an eSIM from: ✔️ Airalo ✔️ Holafly ✔️ ByteSIM  If you prefer a physical SIM card, go to an official Turkcell or Türk Telekom store and NOT airport kiosks to get a local plan with internet.  5️⃣ Ignoring street food because it's not "Instagrammable" 🍽️  Some of the BEST food in Istanbul comes from places that don't look fancy! My top 5 street food spots: 🍕 Pide: Nizam Pide Taksim 🐟 Balık Ekmek: Meşhur Tarihi Eminönü 🥩 Döner Kebab: Dürümce Taksim 🥟 İçli Köfte: Hayri Usta Ocakbaşı 🍬 Baklava: Sakarya Tatlıcısı

column14: THE ONLY WAY OUT IS THROUGH
column15: The transformative nature of kindness  It started when this 1914 Robert Frost quote got stuck in my head —  "The best way out is always through."  It felt like such a perfect reminder that difficult situations cannot be resolved through denial and healing cannot happen through brute force. Moving through any difficult situation requires tenderness and vulnerability.  And then I started to imagine, what if the fight can't actually be fought? What if the only way through anything is through forgiveness - of both ourselves and others? What if the thing our younger self really needs isn't yet another round of scolding and criticism, but a hug? And what if our angry neighbor didn't need a cold word, but some company?  It's also a reminder I suppose that we are all wounded in our own ways, and meeting violence with tenderness can be incredibly transformative. The best way to emerge from struggle is by moving through it — facing it head-on with your heart open. It's a brave, painful, wonderful thing. column14: TYPES OF INSULIN  RAPID ACTING i.e. Novorapid, Humalog Onset: 10-20 mins Peak: 1-3 hours Duration: 2-5 hours Works quickly and is taken just before or right after meals. It helps control the rapid rise in blood glucose levels which occurs with eating  SHORT ACTING i.e. Actrapid, Humulin S Onset: 30-60 mins Peak: 1-5 hours Duration: 5-9 hours Mimics the normal physiologcal response of insulin secretion which occurs after eating  INTERMEDIATE ACTING i.e. Insulatard, Humulin I Onset: 60-90 mins Peak: 4-8 hours Duration: 10-18 hours Often used to provide background insulin throughout the day as it provides an extended coverage  LONG ACTING i.e. Levemir, Lantus, Levemir Onset: 2-4 hours Peak: NO PEAK Duration: 24-36 hours Provides a slow and steady release of insulin over an extended period. Often used to maintain blood glucose levels between meals & overnight
column15: Types of insulin - a student nurses overview  1. Rapid-acting insulin  -Mimics natural insulin release with meals -Must be administered just before or with food -Often used in bolus insulin regimens (along-side long-acting)  2. Short-acting insulin  -Used for meal coverage but with a slower onset than rapid-acting insulin -Administer 30 minutes before meals -Can be given IV in emergencies (e.g. diabetic ketoacidosis)  3. Intermediate-acting insulin  -Often used as basal insulin for background glucose control -Can be combined with short-acting insulin for better glucose control -Long-acting insulin -Provides steady glucose control throughout the day  4. Long-acting insulin  -Provides steady glucose control throughout the day -No significant peak, reducing the risk of hypoglycaemia

column14: Healthiest brands of Coffee Creamer  From a dietitian nutritionist
column15: When choosing the best coffee creamer, I look for: 1. Ingredient Quality - Avoid hydrogenated oils, artificial sweeteners, and excessive additives. Look for clean, whole-food ingredients. 2. Sugar Content - Limit added sugars to prevent blood sugar spikes and excess calorie intake. Opt for unsweetened or naturally sweetened options. 3. Protein & Nutrients - Creamers with added protein (like collagen or whey) or functional ingredients (like MCT oil) can support metabolism and satiety. 4. Gut & Digestive Health - Choose options with probiotics or gut-friendly fats and avoid emulsifiers that may disrupt gut microbiota. 5. Dairy-Free & Plant-Based Options - Consider almond, oat, or coconut creamers for those with lactose intolerance or dietary preferences, but check for added gums and oils. 6. Taste & Texture - A good creamer should enhance coffee without overpowering its flavor, maintaining a creamy and smooth consistency.

column14: I lost my dad when I was 23 and I'm now 26.  Death and grief are things we should talk about more. I was scared of death before my Dad passed, but when it touches your life your outlook changes.
column15: George's story 💭  Today we’re sharing George’s story. In this blog post, George shares some of his reflections on grief since losing his dad at 23.  Grief can often feel isolating, especially for young adults, and often more so for young men. Yet, 1 in 7 people will experience the loss of a parent before turning 30.  Despite this, the conversation around grief still remainsa taboo. At It’s Time, we believe in the power of sharing stories from our community. By opening up about our experiences, we not only feel connected those we’ve lost, but we also remind each other that it’s okay to grieve, to talk about the person we miss, and to seek connection. Sharing these stories is a way to help others feel seen, understood, and less alone.  Click the link to ‘real stories’ in our bio to read George’s story 💭

column14: 5 Badass Roman Quotes for No Excuses Mentality @Rare_Breed_Mentality
column15: 5 Badass quotes for no excuses mentality  Badass Roman quotes 	1.	“Alea iacta est.” “The die is cast.” — Julius Caesar (Crossing the Rubicon, signaling a point of no return.) 	2.	“Veni, vidi, vici.” “I came, I saw, I conquered.” — Julius Caesar (After a swift victory in battle.) 	3.	“Si vis pacem, para bellum.” “If you want peace, prepare for war.” — Vegetius (Military strategy emphasizing strength as deterrence.) 	4.	“Audentes fortuna iuvat.” “Fortune favors the bold.” — Virgil (Encouraging bravery in the face of risk.) 	5.	“Memento mori.” “Remember you must die.” — Stoic reminder of mortality and urgency to act. 	6.	“Aut viam inveniam aut faciam.” “I will either find a way or make one.” — Hannibal (Demonstrating determination in adversity.) 	7.	“Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.” “It is sweet and fitting to die for one’s country.” — Horace (Roman ideal of patriotism and sacrifice.) 	8.	“Non ducor, duco.” “I am not led, I lead.” - A statement of leadership and independence. 9. "Gladiator in arena consilium capit." "The gladiator makes his plan in the arena." - Refers to learning and adapting through action. 10. "Faber est suae quisque fortunae." ”Every man is the architect of his own fortune." - Sallust (Emphasizing personal responsibility for success.)

column14: ChatGPT Tips A prompt to avoid ChatGPT simply agreeing with everything you say. [Save for later]
column15: A prompt to avoid ChatGPT to simply agreeing with everything you say. ChatGPT agreeing with everything you say? That's not helpful. Use this prompt to make it challenge your ideas, point out flaws, and give better answers. A prompt to avoid ChatGPT simply agreeing with everything you say "From now on, do not simply affirm my statements or assume my conclusions are correct. Your goal is to be an intellectual sparring partner, not just on agreeable assistant. Every time I present an idea, do the following: 1. Analyze my assumptions. What am I taking for granted that might not be true? 2. Provide counterpoints. What would an intelligent, well-informed skeptic say in response? 3. Test my reasoning. Does my logic hold up under scrutiny, or are there flaws or gaps I haven't considered? 4. Offer alternative perspectives. How else might this idea be framed, interpreted, or challenged? 5. Prioritize truth over agreement. If I am wrong or my logic is weak, I need to know. Correct me clearly and explain why." "Maintain a constructive, but rigorous, approach. Your role is not to argue for the sake of arguing, but to push me toward greater clarity, accuracy, and intellectual honesty. If I ever start slipping into confirmation bias or unchecked assumptions, call it out directly. Let's refine not just our conclusions, but how we arrive at them." Check out my collection of useful prompts to get the most out of Al!

Handstand Hold (Against Wall) – 3 sets x 20-30 seconds 🟢Build up your time gradually as you get stronger.  5. Backpack Shoulder Press – 4 sets x 10-12 reps 🟢Fill a backpack with books or bottles and press it overhead.
